segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of the Men's High Jump Final at the Rio 2016 Olympics, set in a large, brightly lit stadium at night. Upbeat, rhythmic drumming from a group of drummers in red and white uniforms near the landing mat adds a festive atmosphere, as a male commentator announces, "Men's High Jump Final night at the Olympic Stadium." The camera pans across officials, photographers, and athletes, with on-screen graphics displaying the event title and Olympic rings. The view shifts to a closer angle of the high jump setup, where a man in a gray hoodie walks near the mat, while the commentator describes it as "a really exciting field... always an exciting competition... they are so beautiful to watch the high jumpers." On-screen graphics then transition to display the world record (2.45m by Javier Sotomayor, Cuba, 1993) and Olympic record (2.39m by Charles Austin, USA, 1996), with the commentator detailing, "The bar will start at 2.20... it could get beyond 2.40 by the time it finishes. And there's Sotomayor's world record of 2.45 and the last of the Americans to win was Austin Charles, Charles Austin back in Atlanta," speculating that "the 2.39, his 2.39 will be challenged." The start list for the final is displayed, listing 15 athletes from various nations, as the commentator introduces "Brendan Starc here of Australia," noting his previous performance. The camera then cuts to a close-up of Erik Kynard of the USA in a blue jacket, covering his mouth in a gesture of focus, as the commentator highlights him as "Kynard of the United States, really comes into here as one of the favorites," and describes the field as "probably the most open field I've seen in a major high jump or an Olympic Games in a long time." This is followed by a close-up of Derek Drouin of Canada in a red jacket, smiling confidently. The final shot shows Derek Drouin in his black and red competition uniform with 'CANADA' and 'DROUIN' on the front, walking along the track, preparing for his jump amidst spectators and flags, while the commentator emphasizes the importance of "form on the night... rhythm... making sure that they get approach to the bar correct," and the "subtle adjustments they will need to make with their run-ups" as the bar goes higher, concluding by mentioning "one of the veterans now from..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Derek Drouin, the Canadian high jumper, introduced by commentators as being 33 years of age. He wears his black and red uniform with 'CANADA' and 'DROUIN' visible, appearing focused as on-screen graphics confirm his first attempt at 2.20m. Commentators describe his 'very unusual take-off,' noting he looks 'more like a long jumper' with his arm use and is a 'very rigid jumper' lacking flexibility. As he begins his run-up along the blue track, the crowd's murmur is audible. He executes a powerful Fosbury Flop, clearing the white bar with precision. Commentators exclaim, "Well, he's got the clearance though, he's got the height! He was way above that!" praising his 'outstanding' and 'different style.' Drouin lands safely on the blue mat and walks away calmly and confidently. The scene then shifts to another athlete in a red uniform, identified by commentators as 'one of the favorites' from Qatar. He attempts the same height, but knocks the bar off with his back, resulting in a failed attempt. Commentators note he 'did not waste any time at all' and 'decided to go at the 2.20 and not pass,' suggesting he 'just wanted to have some fun.' The visibly disappointed athlete walks away as officials raise a red flag. The segment concludes with a wide shot of the competition area, showing the High Jump Apparatus, the landing mat, and the surrounding Stadium Environment with Rio 2016 branding, while commentators discuss strategy.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a commentator reflecting on the 'great respect for the sport' and an athlete's 'feature of concentration' in a 'private moment' before a jump, as the crowd's anticipation builds. A distinct 'thud' sound is heard, followed by the commentator declaring, 'Not this time for Drouin,' clarifying that his final attempt was a 'first mistake' but 'didn't matter' as it was 'only for fun,' chasing an Olympic record. Visually, Derek Drouin of Canada, in his black and red uniform, is then seen celebrating his victory with arms raised and a broad smile, as the on-screen graphic confirms him as the 'WINNER' with 2.38m. The commentator emphatically announces, 'Derek Drouin of Canada wins the Men's High Jump Rio 2016,' and 'he is the winner, the gold medalist.' The camera cuts to the enthusiastic Canadian fan group in the stands, waving flags and cheering loudly, their excitement palpable. Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar, in a red jacket and cap, is then shown smiling and raising his arms in celebration for securing second place with a 2.36m jump, as confirmed by the on-screen graphic. Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, in his blue and yellow uniform, is seen stretching and preparing to leave after finishing third with a 2.33m jump, also displayed on the graphic. The vibrant stadium environment, illuminated by bright lights with Rio 2016 branding and the OMEGA timer visible, reinforces the competitive atmosphere. The commentator continues to analyze Drouin's technique, noting he is 'unorthodox, but he's very effective,' and 'more upright,' as the segment concludes with a wide shot of the celebratory stadium.

The OMEGA timer and official scoreboards are visible, reinforcing the competitive atmosphere. The segment concludes with a wide shot of the stadium, capturing the celebratory mood and the athletes' post-competition interactions.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of an athlete in a red uniform kneeling on the blue landing mat, gathering his red cap and white towel, as a photographer captures the moment. The scene then transitions to a wide shot of the stadium, where the official results for the Men's High Jump Final are displayed on a large on-screen graphic. A commentator confirms the results, stating, "And there the minor medalist Bondarenko with the bronze," followed by "Barshim takes the silver." The graphic shows Derek Drouin of Canada in first place with 2.38m, Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar in second with 2.36m, and Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine in third with 2.33m. The commentator further clarifies, "And just confirming the results, Derek Drouin of Canada, 2 meters 38. Barshim of Qatar on the count back beats Bondarenko, he takes the silver medal. Bondarenko the bronze." The graphic scrolls to reveal additional competitors, while the commentator praises the athletes, noting, "all of these athletes should be incredibly proud of themselves," and that "these top 15 go higher than anybody else." He concludes by hailing "Another outstanding Olympic Games high jump competition," adding, "This time, it is the Maple Leaf who will stand on the highest step of the Olympic podium." The camera then pans across the now empty high jump area, showing the green-poled apparatus and the blue 'Rio 2016' landing mat, with officials and staff walking around the track amidst the continued crowd murmurs. The final shot shows a man in a red shirt, likely a volunteer, walking past benches and recycling bins, under the stadium lights with the crowd visible in the background.

The camera then pans across the empty high jump area, showing the green-poled apparatus and the blue 'Rio 2016' landing mat, as officials and staff walk around the track. The final shot shows a man in a red shirt and black pants, likely a volunteer or official, walking past white benches and green recycling bins near the track, with the stadium lights illuminating the night sky and the crowd visible in the background.
